issue_title: Poor label quality: "Subscribe to our mailing list"
actual_result: The label describes the purpose of the form/action ('Subscribe to our mailing list') rather than describing what data the user should enter. It functions as a heading or call-to-action, not as a field label.; A user relying on a screen reader would hear 'Subscribe to our mailing list' for an email input field but would not be explicitly told that an email address is expected.; The actual data format hint ('Email') is relegated to the placeholder attribute, which disappears on input and is not a reliable substitute for a descriptive label.
expected_result: Form field labels should be descriptive and meaningful
recommendation: Change the visible label to 'Email address' (or 'Email') so it clearly describes the expected input. The surrounding context or a heading above the field can still convey 'Subscribe to our mailing list' as the form's purpose. For example, use a form heading 'Subscribe to our mailing list' and label the field 'Email address'.

issue_title: Required field not clearly indicated: "Subscribe to our mailing list"
actual_result: The field is programmatically required (required HTML attribute) but the effective label 'Subscribe to our mailing list' does not indicate that the field is required (e.g., no '*' symbol or '(required)' text).; There are no instructions (aria-describedby text) that communicate the required status to the user.; The required nature of this field is conveyed only programmatically, which some assistive technologies may not surface clearly and which is invisible to sighted users.; The label 'Subscribe to our mailing list' describes the purpose of the form rather than the expected input; it does not clearly communicate that an email address is expected. The placeholder 'Email' provides this hint, but placeholders disappear on input and are not a reliable substitute for a visible label.
expected_result: Required field status should be communicated visually and programmatically
recommendation: Update the effective label to clearly indicate both the expected input and the required status. For example, change the label to 'Email address *' or 'Email address (required)' and add a visible legend or note (e.g., '* indicates required field') near the form. Additionally, consider adding descriptive instructions via aria-describedby such as 'Required. Enter your email address to subscribe to our mailing list.' to ensure the requirement is communicated both visually and to assistive technology users. Do not rely solely on the placeholder text to convey the type of input expected.

actual_result: The filename 'Shakuntala-_poster_fb.jpg' strongly suggests this is a poster image — likely a movie, play, or event poster for 'Shakuntala.' Posters are inherently informational, conveying details such as the title, visual branding, and promotional content. Even without surrounding text, a poster image carries meaningful content that a screen reader user would miss.
expected_result: Image marked as decorative (alt="") should truly be decorative
recommendation: Add descriptive alt text that conveys the key information shown on the poster, e.g., the title, event type, and any other prominent text or imagery visible in the poster.

actual_result: The filename 'VisionAidBanner-800x600-1.png' indicates this is a banner image for 'VisionAid,' which appears to be an organization or initiative. Banners typically contain branding, messaging, calls to action, or other informational content that is critical for understanding the page's purpose. The name 'VisionAid' itself suggests an accessibility-related organization, making proper alt text even more important from an inclusivity standpoint.
expected_result: Image marked as decorative (alt="") should truly be decorative
recommendation: Add alt text that describes the banner's content, including the organization name 'VisionAid,' any tagline, call to action, or key message displayed in the banner image.

actual_result: Missing role="img" on the SVG element; Missing <title> element to provide a text alternative; Missing aria-labelledby attribute to associate the SVG with a <title>; No aria-label provided as an alternative accessible name; SVG has no accessible name whatsoever, failing WCAG 1.1.1 (Non-text Content, Level A)
expected_result: SVGs should have role="img" and an accessible name via title + aria-labelledby
recommendation: Add role="img" to the <svg> element. Add a <title> element with a unique id and meaningful, descriptive text as the first child of the <svg>. Then reference that id via aria-labelledby on the <svg>. Optionally, add a <desc> element for a longer description if the graphic conveys complex information. Example: <svg role="img" aria-labelledby="svg-title-1"><title id="svg-title-1">Descriptive title of the graphic</title><desc>Optional longer description</desc>...</svg>. If the SVG is purely decorative, mark it with aria-hidden="true" and role="presentation" instead.

actual_result: Icon is the sole content of a link/button but is hidden from screen readers via aria-hidden="true"; The interactive control has no accessible name, violating WCAG 1.1.1 and 4.1.2
expected_result: Icon fonts should be properly labeled or hidden from assistive technology
recommendation: Remove aria-hidden="true" from the icon and add an aria-label (e.g., aria-label="Search") to the parent or

actual_result: Icon has no aria-hidden="true" and no aria-label or visible text; Screen readers may announce meaningless content (e.g., a Unicode glyph or nothing useful)
expected_result: Icon fonts should be properly labeled or hidden from assistive technology
recommendation: If the icon is decorative or supplementary to a parent control, add aria-hidden="true". If it conveys meaning (e.g., indicating a dropdown), add an aria-label such as aria-label="Toggle dropdown" or add visually-hidden text. Ensure the parent control has an accessible name.
